@graph.raw_description: The narrator's father’s family name is revealed to be Pirrip
The narrator's Christian name is revealed to be Philip
The narrator's infant tongue could make of both names nothing longer or more explicit than Pip
The narrator called himself Pip
The narrator came to be called Pip
The narrator gives Pirrip as his father's family name
The narrator states that he never saw his father or mother
The narrator states that he never saw any likeness of either of them
The narrator reflects on his first fancies regarding what his parents were like
The narrator derives his fancies about his parents from their tombstones

@graph.raw_description: The narrator describes the shape of the letters on his father's tombstone
The narrator has an odd idea that his father was a square, stout, dark man with curly black hair
The narrator drew a childish conclusion about his mother.
The narrator perceived that his mother was freckled and sickly.
Five little stone lozenges were arranged in a neat row beside the grave
The lozenges were sacred to the memory of five little brothers of Philip Pirrip
Philip Pirrip believed that his brothers had all been born on their backs
Philip Pirrip believed that his brothers had their hands in their trousers-pockets
Philip Pirrip believed that his brothers had never taken their hands out in this state of existence
Philip Pirrip reflected on the early death of his brothers
The narrator gained a vivid and broad impression of the identity of things
The narrator found out for certain that the bleak place overgrown with nettles was the churchyard
The narrator found out that Philip Pirrip, late of this parish, and Georgiana, wife of the above, were dead and buried
The narrator found out that Alexander, Bartholomew, Abraham, Tobias, and Roger, infant children of the aforesaid, were also dead and buried
The narrator found out that the dark flat wilderness beyond the churchyard was the marshes
The narrator found out that the low leaden line beyond was the river
The narrator found out that the distant savage lair from which the wind was rushing was the sea
Pip was a small bundle of shivers growing afraid of it all and beginning to cry
A man started up from among the graves at the side of the church porch
A man cried a terrible voice
A man threatened to cut the throat of a little devil
Philip Pirrip was called a little devil
A fearful man appeared, all in coarse grey, with a great iron on his leg
The man had no hat
The man wore broken shoes
The man had an old rag tied round his head

The man instructed to point out the place
